This protein family exhibits type I GTP cyclohydrolase activity, catalyzing the first step in tetrahydrofolate biosynthesis in bacteria or methanopterin biosynthesis in archaea. These enzymes convert GTP into dihydroneopterin derivatives, utilizing zinc as a cofactor.

Ammonium transporters facilitate the uptake of ammonia or ammonium, and in some cases methylammonium, via a two-lane pathway where NH3 and H+ are transported separately before recombining. They also function as sensors of extracellular ammonium concentration.
The RDR6 N-terminal domain is an RNA-binding domain involved in gene silencing and virus resistance, possessing polymerase activity and being required for siRNA production.

The second domain of plant RDR6, similar to PH domains, likely forms a β-barrel structure and is involved in siRNA production, post-transcriptional gene silencing, and virus resistance via ssRNA/ssDNA-dependent polymerase activity.

This protein family includes phosphoglycerate mutase (PGM) homologs, some of which catalyze phosphoglycerate interconversion in carbohydrate metabolism, while others may have alternative functions like sterol transport.

TRM11 is a tRNA methyltransferase subunit that catalyzes guanosine methylation at position 10 in tRNA, utilizing S-adenosyl-L-methionine as a methyl donor. Methyltransferases are a diverse enzyme class involved in many cellular processes.

The enoylreductase (ER) domain is a catalytic domain found in polyketide synthases (PKSs) and fatty acid synthases (FAS), where it reduces 2-enoyl intermediates during polyketide and fatty acid biosynthesis. It is also present in mitochondrial fatty acid synthesis enzymes and some dehydrogenases.
Vma21 is an endoplasmic reticulum protein essential for the assembly of the V0 membrane sector of the V-ATPase.

This protein family includes phosphatase regulators, which modulate PP2A and PP4 activity and complex formation, and methionine-R-sulfoxide reductases, which protect against oxidative stress by reducing methionine sulfoxide.

This protein family participates in the transcription and processing of snRNAs, which are crucial for pre-mRNA splicing, and forms part of the Integrator complex that interacts with RNA polymerase II. They may also be involved in intracellular transport by recruiting cytoplasmic dynein.
This family of chromatin modification proteins, found in diverse organisms, regulates cell cycle, apoptosis, and DNA repair via C-terminal PHD finger domains involved in transcriptional regulation.
The BURP domain is a conserved C-terminal motif found in plant cell wall proteins, potentially involved in protein localization within the cell wall matrix and mediating intermolecular interactions.
This entry describes subunits of chromatin remodeling complexes, specifically RSC in yeast and SWI/SNF in its paralog, involved in altering chromatin structure.

The polyketide transferase af380 family enzymes catalyze the transfer of polyketide chains in the biosynthesis of diverse secondary metabolites, including pharmaceutically relevant compounds. Some members also function as thiohydrolases, potentially involved in polyketide chain release and macrolactonization.

CWC26 family proteins are spliceosome components involved in pre-mRNA splicing, including the removal of U12-type introns. In yeast, they are also linked to pre-mRNA nuclear retention and bud site selection.

Gluten, the protein component of wheat flour, consists of glutenins (responsible for elasticity) and gliadins (responsible for extensibility), both containing repetitive sequences that form loose helical structures. Glutenins form an elastic network through disulfide bonds and helical structures, while gliadins have more extensive non-repetitive globular regions.

This C-terminal domain in ENGase, predicted to have an Ig-like fold, is part of a glycosyl hydrolase that cleaves N-glycosylated proteins, playing a role in cytosolic degradation and sugar recycling. It belongs to the glycosyl hydrolase family 85 (GH85).
Cytochrome b-c1 complex subunit 7 (QCR7/UQCRB) is a component of the mitochondrial cytochrome b-c1 complex (complex III) and is involved in redox-linked proton pumping.
Broad-minded protein, in complex with CCRK, regulates ciliary structure, while PHAF1, with BCAS3, influences autophagy by recruiting proteins to the phagophore assembly site.

Penicillin-binding protein 5/6 C-terminal domain is a structural motif found in bacterial D-Ala-D-Ala carboxypeptidases, potentially mediating interactions with cell wall synthesis enzymes and positioning the active site for peptidoglycan interaction.

MttB/TatC proteins are integral membrane components of the twin-arginine translocation (Tat) system, essential for recognizing signal peptides and translocating proteins across the membrane in bacteria. They contain multiple transmembrane helices, with the N and C termini exposed to the cytoplasm.

Sirtuin proteins are a conserved family of NAD-dependent deacetylases involved in transcriptional silencing, cell cycle progression, and chromosome stability. They possess a catalytic core domain with a Rossmann fold that binds NAD, enabling deacetylation of histones and other targets.
This entry describes a cellulose-binding domain (CBD) found in fungal cellulases and xylanases, which facilitates the binding of these enzymes to cellulose via conserved disulfide bonds.

The GatC subunit is part of the aspartyl/glutamyl-tRNA amidotransferase complex (GatABC) that catalyzes the formation of correctly charged Asn-tRNA(Asn) or Gln-tRNA(Gln) by transamidating misacylated Asp-tRNA(Asn) or Glu-tRNA(Gln) in organisms lacking asparaginyl-tRNA or glutaminyl-tRNA synthetases.

Ribosomal protein bL31 is a component of the large ribosomal subunit, found in bacteria and chloroplasts, and is essential for mRNA-directed protein synthesis. Ribosomal proteins like bL31 stabilize rRNA structure and may have functions outside the ribosome.
GSCR2 is a human protein similar to a glioma tumor suppressor candidate. Nop53 is a yeast nucleolar protein involved in ribosome biogenesis and pre-rRNA processing.

The biotin/lipoyl attachment domain binds biotin or lipoic acid via a conserved lysine residue and adopts a flattened beta-barrel structure. These domains are crucial for enzymatic activity in carboxyl transfer reactions (biotin) and 2-oxo acid dehydrogenase complexes (lipoic acid).

This family includes aldehyde dehydrogenases involved in diverse metabolic pathways, such as D-sulfoquinovose degradation, taurine utilization, F420 biosynthesis, and potentially phosphonate degradation. These enzymes exhibit substrate specificity and are regulated by metabolites to fulfill specific cellular roles.
This domain, found in DNA methyltransferases (DNMTs), non-catalytically targets the enzyme to replication foci, enabling proper methylation and interaction with DMAP1 and HDAC2.

This domain, found in NSD histone-lysine methyltransferases, forms a PHD finger fold that interacts with an atypical PHD finger to mediate protein-protein interactions and determine histone binding specificity.
This protein family includes bacterial and eukaryotic outer membrane proteins involved in protein assembly and translocation, including components of the BAM, SAM, and TOC complexes.
PIG-T is a component of the GPI transamidase complex, essential for attaching GPI anchors to proteins. It likely forms a core complex with other subunits to facilitate this process.

Fringe proteins are glycosyltransferases that modify Notch receptor EGF repeats, modulating Notch signaling by differentially affecting its interaction with Delta and Serrate/Jagged ligands, thereby influencing various developmental processes. They utilize UDP-N-acetylglucosamine as a donor substrate and require manganese for activity.
POC5 is a centrin-binding protein essential for building the distal half of centrioles, but not for initiating their assembly.
The KASH5 EF-hand-like domain is part of the LINC complex, which connects the nuclear lamina to the cytoskeleton and mediates telomere localization through interaction with SUN1.
This family includes fungal transmembrane proteins involved in resistance to toxins and transport of sphingoid long-chain bases.

Glycosyltransferase family 31 (GT31) comprises enzymes that catalyze the transfer of sugar moieties to form glycosidic bonds, with known activities including N-acetyllactosaminide beta-1,3-N-acetylglucosaminyltransferase and beta-1,3-galactosyltransferase. GT31 enzymes are involved in the biosynthesis of disaccharides, oligosaccharides, and polysaccharides.

This protein family belongs to the haloacid dehalogenase superfamily and functions as a phosphatase, catalyzing the dephosphorylation of both glucosyl-3-phosphoglycerate and mannosyl-3-phosphoglycerate.

The ATL subfamily of MGMT proteins recognizes and repairs DNA damage, preferentially binding to O(6)-alkylguanine adducts and flipping the damaged base for nucleotide excision repair. They also influence the DNA repair pathway choice based on the size of the adduct.

The RPW8 domain is found in plant disease resistance proteins and is involved in broad-spectrum mildew resistance via salicylic acid-dependent defenses. It contains a transmembrane region/signal peptide and a coiled-coil motif.
This family includes transmembrane proteins with coiled-coil domains found across kingdoms, some of which possess an alpha/beta hydrolase domain.

3-ketodihydrosphingosine reductases (KDSRs) are oxidoreductases that catalyze the reduction of 3-ketodihydrosphingosine to dihydrosphingosine in sphingolipid biosynthesis, featuring a conserved Rossmann fold and active site motifs characteristic of the SDR family.
Ard1 is the catalytic subunit of the Ard1-Nat1 complex, a conserved N-terminal acetyltransferase that modifies newly synthesized proteins.

This protein family includes archaeal flagellins and related bacterial sequences, which are components of the archaeal flagella system responsible for motility. Archaeal flagellins, similar to bacterial type IV pilins, are synthesized with a leader peptide that is cleaved by a peptidase.

The DZIP/RILPL protein family regulates cilia assembly and function, cellular transport, and cell polarity. DZIP members act as adaptors for protein complexes at the cilium basal body, while RILPL proteins regulate cell shape, protein transport, and neuroprotection.
Long-chain chromate transporters reduce chromate accumulation, providing chromate resistance, and likely originated from a gene fusion of two short-chain transporters.

Peptidase C19 family (ubiquitin carboxyl-terminal hydrolases) are deubiquitinating enzymes involved in protein turnover, DNA repair, cell cycle, apoptosis, immunity, and signaling pathways. They process/recycle ubiquitin, edit polyubiquitin chains, and prevent proteasomal degradation, influencing processes like mitophagy and circadian rhythm.
This domain, found in plant telomere repeat-binding proteins, likely adopts a ubiquitin-like fold and binds plant telomeric DNA, influencing telomere length and development.
This domain is found in bacterial, viral, and mammalian proteins, and some members may function as phosphodiester glycosidases.

Ferredoxins are small electron transfer proteins containing iron-sulfur clusters (4Fe-4S, 3Fe-4S, or 2Fe-2S) that participate in redox reactions. The 2Fe-2S ferredoxin domain, found in various metabolic enzymes, adopts a β-grasp fold and coordinates iron atoms via cysteine residues.

TIM50 family proteins are essential components of the TIM23 complex, mediating the translocation of transit peptide-containing proteins across the mitochondrial inner membrane and facilitating protein transfer from the TOM complex. They play a dynamic role in protein import.
This entry describes a conserved C-terminal region, PPW-GFGT motif, found in a PPE protein subfamily.
This domain, named Gate, determines nucleoside specificity in some transporters and forms the membrane pore in others, suggesting a broader role in substrate selectivity and transport.

Arginine dihydrolase (AD) pathway, found in prokaryotes and some eukaryotes, breaks down L-arginine to produce ATP, carbon dioxide, and ammonia. The repressor protein ArgR regulates this pathway by binding to DNA and modulating arginine biosynthesis or catabolism.

This protein family functions as isomerases in bacilysin biosynthesis, catalyzing the conversion of dihydro-hydroxyphenylpyruvate isomers, which is crucial for bacilysin's antibiotic activity against glucosamine synthetase.

The trefoil (TFF) domain is a cysteine-rich structural motif found in extracellular eukaryotic proteins, characterized by a three-leafed structure formed by three disulfide bonds, and is implicated in tissue protection and repair.
This uncharacterized Bacteroidia protein family is suggested to be a pilus component, potentially anchoring the pilus complex to the bacterial surface.

This conserved kinase domain is found in phosphatidylinositol-4-phosphate 5-kinases (PIP5K) and 1-phosphatidylinositol-3-phosphate 5-kinases, which are involved in phosphoinositide signaling by phosphorylating phosphoinositides.
This family of conserved eukaryotic membrane proteins, including Tapt1, is implicated in skeletal patterning and Golgi/ER function, potentially related to protein folding and trafficking.
This entry describes the Mu homology domain (MHD)-like domain found at the C-terminus of fungal vacuolar membrane proteases, such as PFF1, which are involved in vacuolar sorting and osmoregulation.

This entry describes the transmembrane domain of fungal vacuolar membrane proteases, such as yeast PFF1, which are involved in vacuolar sorting and osmoregulation and contain a M28 peptidase domain and a Mu homology domain (MHD)-like fold.

Endoplasmic reticulum metallopeptidase 1 (ERMP1) is an endoplasmic reticulum membrane protein that may be involved in protein processing for cellular organization. Homologs like yeast PFF1 may function in vacuolar sorting and osmoregulation.

Akirin is an adapter protein essential for embryonic development and innate immunity, linking chromatin remodeling complexes to transcription factors. Akirin-2 mediates nuclear protein degradation by importing proteasomes into the nucleus via interaction with import receptor IPO9.

This entry describes the conserved N-terminal region of the eukaryotic TFIIE-alpha subunit and related archaeal proteins, which are essential for mRNA transcription initiation. The TFIIE β-central core domain binds double-stranded DNA via a novel positively charged furrow, contributing to promoter opening during transcription.

This protein family comprises NADH:flavin oxidoreductases and NADH oxidases involved in diverse redox reactions, utilizing NAD(P)H to reduce flavin or other substrates. They participate in metabolic processes like fatty acid beta-oxidation, bile acid degradation, and metal ion reduction.

NF-YA is a subunit of the CCAAT-binding factor (CBF) or nuclear transcription factor Y (NF-Y), a heteromeric transcription factor that binds the CCAAT box in eukaryotic gene promoters and enhancers. NF-YA contains an essential core region with an N-terminal subunit-association domain and a C-terminal DNA recognition domain.
This family includes integral membrane proteins, such as Rnf complex subunits and Na+-NQR subunits, that function as ferredoxin:NAD+ oxidoreductases, potentially translocating protons or sodium ions.
Rfa1/RPA70 is a subunit of the RPA complex, a key factor in DNA replication, repair, recombination, and telomere maintenance through its ssDNA binding and structure modulation activity.

Nucleoporins are key components of the nuclear pore complex that mediate transport of molecules like mRNA and proteins. This entry represents the N-terminal domain of several nucleoporins (including Nup133, Nup155, Nup15, and Nup170), which forms a seven-bladed β propeller structure.

The Sec23/24 heterodimer is a component of the COPII coat complex, which mediates cargo selection during vesicle formation at the endoplasmic reticulum (ER); its β-barrel domain contributes to the inner and membrane-distal surfaces of the coat.
The Sec23/24 trunk domain, found in COPII coat proteins, has a vWA topology, mediates dimerization, and facilitates interactions with Sar1 during vesicle formation.
This entry represents plant proteins (e.g., TED6/7) involved in secondary cell wall formation in vessel elements.
Noc3 is essential for DNA replication, 60S ribosomal subunit synthesis, and pre-ribosome transport in yeast, with mammalian homologs also regulating adipocyte differentiation.

This entry represents insect and vertebrate histone H3-K79 methyltransferases, homologous to yeast Dot1, which regulate gene expression via histone methylation and are involved in telomeric silencing.

This protein family catalyzes the reversible conversion of acids to acyl-CoA esters, particularly aryl acids, and participates in the degradation of aryl-CoA esters and branched-chain amino acid catabolism, requiring divalent metal cations for activity.

Casein Kinase 1 (CK1) family kinases phosphorylate serine and threonine residues, regulating diverse processes like Wnt signaling, circadian rhythms, and DNA repair. Some isoforms control circadian clock period by phosphorylating PER1/2, while others participate in ciliogenesis, spermatogenesis, and DNA damage responses.

This entry describes the C-terminal domain found in helicases from superfamilies 1 and 2, including eukaryotic translation initiation factor 4A (eIF4A). This domain exhibits a conserved α-β topology similar to other helicases.

The bMERB domain is a Rab effector found in Mical and EHBP proteins, mediating endosomal trafficking by binding to Rab8 family proteins, often with two binding sites for simultaneous Rab binding. It has an all-alpha helical structure.
This domain is present in ATP-dependent enzymes, such as ligases and synthetases, that covalently bind AMP to substrates. It contains a conserved Ser/Thr/Gly-rich region with a Pro-Lys-Gly triplet.

Spen proteins are transcriptional regulators characterized by an N-terminal RNA-binding motif and a C-terminal SPOC domain, which likely mediates protein-protein interactions and repressor complex formation. SPOC domains are found in proteins involved in development, such as Drosophila Split ends and mouse MINT.

PX domains are phosphoinositide-binding modules found in eukaryotic proteins involved in cell signaling, trafficking, and protein sorting. They mediate protein-protein interactions and bind various phosphoinositides, playing a crucial role in protein recruitment and activation.

This domain, found in TADA2A/B and related proteins, is involved in chromatin remodeling and histone acetylation, potentially coordinating these activities by interacting with complexes like ATAC and Swi/Snf.

SANT domains are protein interaction modules found in chromatin remodeling and transcription regulation proteins. They bind histone tails and couple this binding to enzyme catalysis for nucleosome remodeling.

This domain is found in class IV SAM-dependent RNA methyltransferases (TRM10-type), which form homodimers and contain a SPOUT fold with a trefoil knot for SAM binding at the dimer interface. TRM10-type methyltransferases modify tRNA or RNA at the guanine-9 or adenine-9 positions and are found in eukaryotes and archaea.

Small conductance mechanosensitive channels (MscS) form homoheptameric complexes that protect cells against osmotic shock by responding to membrane stretching and depolarization. Each monomer contains a transmembrane region and a cytoplasmic SH3-like barrel domain, forming a channel that opens in response to pressure.
PBZ domains are zinc-finger motifs found in DNA repair proteins like APLF, characterized by a conserved CX5CX6HX5H configuration. They are implicated in DNA metabolism and strand break repair.

Gamma-class carbonic anhydrases catalyze the reversible hydration of carbon dioxide and are important for growth on acetate, likely supporting H(+)/CH(3)COO(-) symport and CO(2) conversion. These enzymes maintain cellular pH and ion balance.
Chlororespiratory reduction 6 is required for the assembly or stabilization of the chloroplast NAD(P)H dehydrogenase complex in Arabidopsis.

Phosphoenolpyruvate carboxylase (PEPCase) is an enzyme that catalyzes the formation of oxaloacetate, playing a key role in carbon fixation in C4 plants and nitrogen fixation in legume root nodules. This entry represents the bacterial and plant family of PEPCases.

This entry describes pre-mRNA 3' end processing proteins, such as yeast Pfs2 and animal WDR33, which are essential components of the cleavage and polyadenylation factor (CPF) complex involved in polyadenylation-dependent pre-mRNA 3'-end formation.
This protein complex functions as a glycine/sarcosine/betaine reductase.

Ras GTPase-activating protein 1 (p120-RasGAP) is a key regulator of the Ras pathway; its N-terminal SH2 domain, along with other domains, mediates diverse functions like apoptosis, proliferation, and cell migration, while its C-terminal domain promotes GTP hydrolysis to deactivate Ras.
LECT2 proteins are liver-specific proteins with a modified metalloendopeptidase fold, potentially involved in hepatocyte growth but catalytically inactive.

Pyridoxal phosphate (PLP) is a versatile coenzyme derived from vitamin B6 that participates in various metabolic reactions, including amino acid biosynthesis and neurotransmitter synthesis. Pyridoxal kinase (PdxK) is essential for PLP synthesis from vitamin B6.

This enzyme family is involved in pantothenic acid biosynthesis by catalyzing the reduction of ketopantoate to pantoic acid, a key step in coenzyme A production, and can be regulated by feedback inhibition. Some members may also contribute to host-selective toxin biosynthesis in plant pathogens.

Mitochondrial distribution and morphology protein 12 (Mdm12) is a component of the ERMES/MDM complex that tethers the endoplasmic reticulum and mitochondria, and is required for mitochondrial outer membrane protein biogenesis and mtDNA maintenance.
This family comprises conserved potassium transporters (KUP, HAK, AtKT) found across diverse species. This entry represents the N-terminal integral membrane domain.
This family includes KUP, HAK, and AtKT potassium transporters and this entry represents the conserved C-terminal domain.

IRE1/2 proteins are transmembrane kinases activated by ER stress, with IRE1 splicing Xbp1 mRNA to initiate the unfolded protein response. IRE2 induces translational repression via ribosomal RNA cleavage in humans, while in mice it regulates DDIT3 expression for unfolded-protein response and apoptosis.

M7 metallopeptidases are Zn-dependent proteases, related to thermolysin, that hydrolyze proteins and contain a conserved HEXXH motif for metal binding, but uniquely utilize an aspartate residue as the third zinc ligand.

This domain mediates myosin binding in vezatin and Inp2, which are involved in peroxisome movement and adhesion. Vezatin links the cadherin-catenin complex to myosin VIIA and is crucial for spermatozoan production.

Laminins are heterotrimeric glycoproteins found in basement membranes that mediate cell interactions via integrins and other receptors. They are crucial for cell differentiation, tissue maintenance, and survival.

This domain is found in alanine dehydrogenase, pyridine nucleotide transhydrogenase, and lysine 2-oxoglutarate reductases, containing a glycine-rich region involved in NAD(H) binding. These enzymes catalyze reversible reactions involving NAD/NADP and amino acids or related compounds.
TOM1-like proteins (TOLs) contain VHS and GAT domains, potentially function in ESCRT complexes for sorting ubiquitinated proteins, and may regulate plant morphogenesis.

SEA domains are extracellular motifs found in diverse cell surface and secreted proteins, often exhibiting autoproteolysis activity and association with extensive O-glycosylation, suggesting roles in protein stabilization and processing.

CUB domains are extracellular structural motifs found in diverse proteins involved in processes like complement activation, development, and tissue repair, often mediating oligomerization, substrate recognition and calcium-binding.

EGF-like domains are extracellular protein modules, often calcium-binding, characterized by six conserved cysteines forming disulfide bonds and a compact fold containing two-stranded β-sheets, mediating protein-protein interactions.
Ly6g6e is a mammalian protein that potentially modulates nicotinic acetylcholine receptor (nAChR) activity.
Claspin is a key protein in the DNA replication checkpoint, facilitating ATR-Chk1 activation in response to DNA damage by interacting with BRCA1. It also has homologues in Drosophila.

Glycosyl hydrolase 5 (cellulase A) family enzymes hydrolyze and metabolize glucan-based polysaccharides, targeting cell wall components and playing roles in cell wall remodeling, nutrient acquisition, and polysaccharide biosynthesis. They exhibit activities such as endoglucanase, beta-glucosidase, and transglycosylase.

ADF/cofilin-like domains are actin-regulatory domains in eukaryotes that enhance actin turnover by interacting with actin monomers and filaments, promoting the disassembly of aged actin filaments. Their concentration-dependent activity involves filament severing and depolymerization at low concentrations, and increased polymerization at high concentrations.
uL23 (L25/L23) is a ribosomal protein of the large subunit, essential for mRNA-directed protein synthesis by stabilizing rRNA structure and streamlining the translation process.

Heparan sulfate acetyl-CoA:alpha-glucosaminide N-acetyltransferase (HGSNAT) is a lysosomal enzyme that acetylates heparan sulfate, a process essential for its catabolism; mutations in HGSNAT cause mucopolysaccharidosis IIIC (MPS IIIC). The catalytic domain of HGSNAT contains a conserved histidine residue critical for enzymatic activity.
This short domain, found in plant proteins like Brevis radix, may regulate cell proliferation/elongation. It is also present in proteins involved in disease resistance and chromosome condensation.

The BREVIS RADIX (BRX) N-terminal domain is a plant transcription factor that modulates growth rate by maintaining brassinosteroid biosynthesis. It regulates cell proliferation and elongation in roots and shoots.

dTDP-4-keto-6-deoxy-d-hexulose 3,5-epimerase (RmlC) is an enzyme involved in bacterial cell wall biosynthesis, converting dTDP-4-keto-6-deoxy-D-glucose to dTDP-4-keto-L-rhamnose via a jelly roll motif active site. It is also involved in the biosynthesis of some antibiotics.

RALBP1/RLIP76 is a Ral-binding GTPase-activating protein involved in endocytosis, cell migration, and metabolite transport, notably transporting glutathione-conjugates and contributing to multidrug resistance.
Ssu72 is a conserved phosphatase involved in transcription, binding TFIIB and participating in mRNA elongation and termination, and is a subunit of the CPF complex.

This family includes NTP pyrophosphatases, such as RdgB and ITPA (HAM1), that hydrolyze non-canonical purine and pyrimidine nucleotides to prevent mutagenesis and regulate nucleotide pools. Some members also function as dihydroneopterin triphosphate diphosphatases in tetrahydrofolate biosynthesis.
The PNT domain, found in Ets transcription factors, is structurally related to SAM domains and mediates protein-protein interactions.

Cytochrome P450 enzymes are a diverse family involved in plant hormone biosynthesis/inactivation, specialized metabolite production for defense, and stress responses, utilizing reactions like hydroxylation and oxidation.

CYP52 proteins are fungal cytochrome P450 mono-oxygenases that catalyze the conversion of fatty acids and alkanes to alpha,omega-dicarboxylic acids. As members of the class E cytochrome P450 proteins, they represent a novel development in Candida yeast.
HupF/HypC proteins are chaperones essential for the post-translational maturation of NiFe-hydrogenase, maintaining the precursor protein in a conformation suitable for nickel incorporation.

This protein family comprises metal-responsive transcriptional regulators that act as repressors of metal resistance or homeostasis operons; metal binding induces dissociation from DNA, activating gene expression for metal stress response. Some members also repress genes involved in biofilm growth, nodulation, and arsenical resistance.
Frataxin is a mitochondrial protein mutated in Friedreich's ataxia, a neurodegenerative disorder. Bacterial homologs (CyaY) likely function in iron-sulfur cluster biosynthesis.

RuvA domain I, featuring an OB-fold structure, is a subunit of the RuvA tetramer that binds Holliday junctions and facilitates DNA base pair rearrangements during homologous recombination in prokaryotes. It interacts with other domains to facilitate the resolution of DNA junctions.
The RuvA C-terminal domain III, found in prokaryotes, regulates Holliday junction branch migration by interacting with RuvB, without directly contacting the DNA.
This family includes eukaryotic proteins homologous to yeast RRN3, a key regulator of rDNA transcription by RNA polymerase I. RRN3 phosphorylation regulates rDNA transcription levels.

Neuromedin U (NmU) is a peptide hormone that stimulates smooth muscle contraction and vasoconstriction. It is derived from a larger precursor protein, with the C-terminal region being highly conserved.

AP complexes are heterotetrameric adaptor proteins that mediate protein transport and clathrin-coated vesicle formation by recruiting clathrin and recognizing sorting signals. Different AP complexes function in distinct trafficking pathways, including endocytosis and endosomal/lysosomal sorting.

Phytochrome A utilizes FHY3 and FAR1, transcription factors homologous to transposases, to activate gene expression and mediate far-red light responses in plants. The FRS family, also derived from transposases, possess a similar domain structure and likely function as transcription factors.
This domain is found in transposases of Mutator-like elements (MULEs) and insertion sequence elements like IS256, suggesting a role in transposition.

The SWIM domain is a zinc-binding motif found in diverse proteins, including E3 ubiquitin ligases, and is crucial for ubiquitination. Zinc finger (Znf) domains are small, versatile motifs that bind DNA, RNA, protein, or lipids and participate in various cellular processes, including transcription and protein folding.

This family comprises prokaryotic aspartate/glutamate leucyltransferases (Bpt) that conjugate leucine to N-terminal aspartate or glutamate residues, playing a role in the N-end rule pathway of protein degradation. Bpt shares sequence similarity with eukaryotic arginyl-transferase Ate1.
Ribosomal biogenesis factor RBIS is essential for the production of both 40S and 60S ribosomal subunits.
This protein family likely contains bifunctional enzymes.
Rad18 is a RING-finger protein in yeast that binds single-stranded DNA, interacts with the Rad6 enzyme, and is essential for DNA lesion bypass during replication.

FliO/MopB family proteins are involved in bacterial motility and virulence by assisting in the localization and assembly of flagellar components and adhesins. These proteins are essential for bacterial movement, host interaction, and pathogenicity.

This protein family includes bacteriochlorophyllide d C-20 methyltransferase (BchU), an enzyme involved in bacteriochlorophyll c production in green sulfur bacteria. BchU is a methyltransferase that uses S-adenosyl L-methionine (SAM) to methylate a variety of biomolecules and is involved in essential cellular processes.
This entry represents plant proteins like rice LAX2, which regulates axillary meristem formation in the nucleus.

This protein family comprises Cas13a endoribonucleases, which are part of the CRISPR system and target/cleave RNA in a crRNA-dependent manner. Upon target RNA binding, these proteins trigger collateral RNA degradation via their two HEPN-like domains.

UvrB is a bacterial DNA repair protein that, as part of the UvrA2B2 complex, recognizes DNA damage, unwinds DNA, and initiates the incision process by recruiting UvrC. It possesses ATPase and helicase-like activity.

RpoE (sigma-24/E/H/AlgU) sigma factors are alternative sigma factors in bacteria and plastids, belonging to the sigma70 family, that regulate the transcription of specific gene subsets by binding to core RNA polymerase.
This protein family includes bacterial outer membrane lipoproteins involved in nutrient uptake and surface antigens in Rickettsia species that likely interact with the host immune system.
Cytoplasmic tRNA 2-thiolation protein 2 (Ncs2/Tuc2/Ctu2) is responsible for 2-thiolation of tRNA wobble positions, potentially through forming a complex with Ctu1.
This domain's function is unknown, but it is often located at the N-terminus of HMG-box domain-containing proteins.
TPGS2 is a component of the neuronal tubulin polyglutamylase complex, which adds glutamate residues to tubulin tails.
Acyl carrier proteins (ACPs) are essential components of fatty acid synthase systems (both type I and II) and polyketide synthases, functioning as carriers of pathway intermediates.
The Rv0428c SH3 domain, found in Mycobacterium tuberculosis, is specific to actinomycetes and may contribute to stress survival, possibly through histone acetyltransferase activity.
The GNAT domain is an acetyltransferase domain found in proteins involved in histone modification (Rv0428c) or arginine biosynthesis (Cgl2740). It catalyzes acetylation reactions using acetyl-CoA.

The WAP domain, characterized by a four-disulfide core, is found in whey acidic proteins and other proteins, often exhibiting antiproteinase activity and diverse functions in processes like metastasis, calcium transport inhibition, and metalloendopeptidase inhibition.

This protein family functions in post-translational modification and molecular chaperoning, with roles in diphthamide biosynthesis for accurate protein synthesis and structural organization in cells. Some members may also bind metal ions like zinc or iron.

This protein family comprises serine/threonine kinases involved in cell cycle, growth, apoptosis, and signaling pathways, regulating diverse cellular processes and responses to stimuli through substrate phosphorylation. They play a crucial role in maintaining cellular integrity, metabolic pathways, immune responses, and development.

This entry describes 2-phospho-L-lactate transferase (CofD) and phosphoenolpyruvate transferase (FbiA), which catalyze the transfer of phospholactate and phosphoenolpyruvate moieties, respectively, to synthesize precursors of F420, a hydride carrier crucial for energy metabolism in methanogens and other organisms. F420 and its precursors also function as cofactors for various enzymes.

MDRs (medium-chain dehydrogenase/reductases) are a diverse family of zinc-dependent alcohol dehydrogenase-like proteins that catalyze a broad range of reactions. They contain an N-terminal catalytic domain and a C-terminal NAD(P) binding Rossmann-fold domain.

Pannexins (vertebrates) and innexins (invertebrates) are gap junction proteins that form channels between cells, facilitating the exchange of ions and small molecules; they are structurally similar to connexins, with four transmembrane segments and cytoplasmic N- and C-terminal domains.

This domain is found in solute-binding proteins of bacterial ABC transporters (family 3), which facilitate the active transport of various solutes, including histidine, lysine/arginine/ornithine, glutamine, and glutamate, across the cytoplasmic membrane, and is also present in some eukaryotic proteins.

SNF2 family proteins are DNA-dependent ATPases involved in chromatin remodeling and epigenetic gene regulation. The DBINO domain, found in the INO80 subfamily, is a conserved DNA-binding domain located near the N-terminus.

TERB2 is a meiosis-specific telomere-associated protein that mediates telomere attachment to the inner nuclear membrane, promoting homologous pairing and synapsis by exchanging telomeric DNA caps. It functions as part of the MAJIN-TERB1-TERB2 complex, which facilitates the release of the shelterin/telosome complex from telomeric DNA during prophase.
This domain is a glutamine/glutamic acid-rich repeat found in involucrin, a protein component of the cornified cell envelope in epithelial cells.

Involucrin is a transglutaminase substrate in keratinocytes that cross-links to membrane proteins, forming an insoluble envelope beneath the plasma membrane. It functions as a glutamyl donor during assembly of the cornified envelope.

This protein family likely functions as lipid hydrolases involved in lipid metabolism, potentially breaking down complex lipids for energy production and signaling. Further research is needed to confirm their specific roles and mechanisms.

Rho/Rac/Cdc42-like GAP domains, also known as BH domains, are found in diverse proteins and regulate Rho GTPases by stimulating their GTPase activity, thereby inactivating them and influencing cell adhesion, motility, and shape. They consist of seven alpha-helices.
No information provided.
This small domain is found in a novel family of nucleolar proteins.
This domain, found in nucleolar proteins like NOL10 and ENP2, is predicted to have an α-β structure and is involved in ribosome biogenesis and nucleolar organization.

The N-terminal domain of NOL10/ENP2 is a β-propeller structure involved in nucleolar organization and ribosome biogenesis. It functions as a structural component and anchor for other nucleolar proteins.
This protein family, found in plants, algae, and cyanobacteria, contains iron-responsive proteins involved in the iron deficiency response.

Wzy is a membrane polymerase in Gram-positive bacteria that polymerizes oligosaccharide repeat subunits to form capsular polysaccharides. It works with a flippase (Wzx) to transport repeat units to the cell surface.

PglL domains are O-oligosaccharyltransferases in Gram-negative bacteria that transfer oligosaccharides to serine or threonine residues of proteins, specifically pilins. They are distinct from WaaL O-antigen ligases.
The ADF-H domain is an actin-binding motif found in ADF/cofilins, twinfilins, and Abp1/drebrins, mediating interactions with actin monomers or filaments and influencing actin dynamics.

6-phosphogluconolactonases (6PGLs) are cytosolic enzymes that catalyze the hydrolysis of 6-phosphogluconolactone to 6-phosphogluconic acid in the pentose phosphate pathway, exhibiting either an α/β hydrolase fold or a 7-bladed β-propeller fold depending on the organism.

This family of fungal zinc finger transcription factors binds DNA and regulates genes involved in metabolism, stress response, development, and cell wall integrity, influencing processes like hyphal growth and virulence.
The RNase H type-II domain is a ribonuclease that cleaves RNA-DNA heteroduplexes and is found in various organisms and retroviruses, playing a role in reverse transcription.
Ribonuclease HIII is an enzyme that cleaves RNA from DNA-RNA hybrids via endonucleolytic cleavage. It is similar to RNase HII in bacteria, with archaeal forms resembling HII more closely.

2,3-bisphosphoglycerate-independent phosphoglycerate mutase (iPGAM) is a metalloenzyme found in eubacteria and plants, containing phosphatase and phosphotransferase domains. The phosphatase domain is similar to alkaline phosphatase and harbors the active site.
MpaB and MpaB' are endoplasmic reticulum oxygenases involved in mycophenolic acid biosynthesis, catalyzing the oxidative cleavage of farnesyl-DHMP.
This domain is a conserved region of Rictor, a component of the mTORC2 complex, which regulates cell growth and motility in response to nutrient and growth-factor signaling.

Rictor is a component of the mTORC2 complex, a Ser/Thr kinase involved in nutrient and growth-factor signaling, and its N-terminal domain regulates mTORC2 activity, impacting cell growth and motility.
The Rictor domain 5 is a conserved region of the Rictor protein, a component of the mTORC2 complex, which regulates cell growth and motility.

PNPLA9 is a calcium-independent phospholipase that hydrolyzes glycerophospholipids, regulating membrane homeostasis, mitochondrial integrity, and signal transduction; mutations are linked to neurodegenerative diseases.
Atp25 is a nuclear protein essential for the expression and assembly of the Atp9 subunit in mitochondrial ATPase.
FtsA is an essential cell division protein that interacts with FtsZ at the septal ring. Its SHS2 domain mediates protein-protein interactions.

SURF1, related to yeast Shy1, is a mitochondrial inner membrane protein involved in cytochrome c oxidase biogenesis. SURF1 defects are linked to Leigh syndrome, a neurological disorder associated with COX deficiency.

SURF1 is a conserved protein involved in cytochrome c oxidase biogenesis, with homologs like Shy1 in yeast. SURF1 defects are linked to Leigh syndrome, a neurological disorder associated with COX deficiency.
NhaD is a bacterial and plant Na(+)/H(+) antiporter that regulates pH and sodium concentrations in bacteria and plant chloroplasts.

NELF-A contains an HDAg-like domain that binds RNA and regulates transcription elongation by interacting with RNAPII and competing with NELF-C/D. The HDAg domain features a helical structure and an extended region, mediating its interaction with RNAPII.
Red1 is a structural component of synaptonemal complexes during meiosis, playing a role in chromosome segregation, meiotic recombination, and checkpoint signaling.

Multi-SAM containing proteins (MSCPs) are animal proteins like ANKS1 and Caskin-1/2, characterized by N-terminal ankyrin repeats and multiple SAM domains; ANKS1 proteins also contain a PTB domain, while Caskin-1/2 contains an SH3 domain and a proline-rich tail.

Serum transport proteins like albumin, alpha-fetoprotein, vitamin D-binding protein, and afamin are evolutionarily related proteins that bind various ligands such as cations, fatty acids, and hormones for transport. Albumin, the main plasma protein, regulates blood osmotic pressure.
SP96 and SP70 are glycoproteins in the slime mold spore coat complex, essential for cellulose binding and proper spore coat assembly.

This EGF-like domain, similar to the follistatin-N-terminal domain, is found in proteins like follistatin (an activin inhibitor) and SPARC/Agrin (involved in cell growth regulation and ECM interaction).

This protein family includes transcriptional regulators with helix-turn-helix motifs that control amino acid biosynthesis genes, and Ahb/Nir enzymes involved in heme biosynthesis, specifically the decarboxylation of siroheme. The activity of these enzymes is modulated by the redox state of heme b.

Glycoside hydrolase family 3 enzymes possess diverse activities, including glucosidase and xylosidase, and this entry represents the C-terminal catalytic domain, potentially involved in beta-glucan binding.
PA14 domains are beta-barrel modules found in diverse proteins, including glycosidases, toxins, and mammalian proteins, and are implicated in carbohydrate binding.
This entry describes an AsnC-like ligand-binding domain found in siroheme decarboxylases.
This entry describes an HTH domain found in siroheme decarboxylase NirdL, an enzyme involved in heme d1 biosynthesis that catalyzes the decarboxylation of siroheme.
This protein family comprises protein-glutamine methyltransferases (HemK) that modify peptide chain release factors, and are typically encoded near the release factor 1 gene (prfA).

GAMETE CELL DEFECTIVE 1 proteins are mitochondrial factors essential for plant fertility, regulating gametophyte development, gene expression, and programmed cell death during reproduction. They also mediate mitochondrial communication between cells in the embryo sac.

Methionine Import ATP-binding proteins are ABC transporter components that mediate energy-dependent uptake of methionine, an essential amino acid, and may also transport other substrates like phosphonates or arginine. They couple ATP hydrolysis to translocate methionine into the cell.

HrcA is a heat-shock repressor protein that regulates the transcription of chaperone genes (dnaK and groE) by binding to the CIRCE element in prokaryotes. This mechanism protects cellular proteins from heat-induced denaturation.

UBR box family E3 ligases recognize N-degrons to mediate ubiquitin-dependent proteolysis via the N-end rule pathway. They contain a conserved UBR box for substrate binding and a RING domain for E3 ligase activity.

The N-end rule relates N-terminal residues to protein half-life and is mediated by the ubiquitin degradation system in eukaryotes. UBR1, containing a UBR-type zinc finger, recognizes destabilizing N-terminal residues (N-degrons) and functions as an E3 ubiquitin ligase in this pathway.
Sds23 is a fungal protein involved in DNA replication, mitosis, sexual development, and response to nutrient deprivation, with phosphorylation potentially regulating its activity.

Beta-hexosaminidase is a glycoside hydrolase family 20 enzyme existing in multiple forms (A, B, and S) with varying subunit compositions involving alpha and beta chains. The enzyme is involved in the hydrolysis of terminal N-acetyl-beta-D-hexosamine residues in glycoconjugates.

Bacterial high-affinity transport systems utilize ABC importers with solute-binding proteins to actively transport solutes across the cytoplasmic membrane. These solute-binding proteins, grouped into families based on sequence similarity, exhibit specificity for different solutes.

MMACHC is a vitamin B12 processing enzyme that catalyzes reductive decyanation and dealkylation reactions, and mutations are linked to methylmalonic aciduria and homocystinuria. It is a divergent NADPH-dependent flavin reductase with glutathione transferase activity.

Topoisomerase VI subunit B is an ATPase component of a heterotetrameric archaeal and plant enzyme that regulates DNA topology by catalyzing transient double-strand breaks and ATP hydrolysis. Topoisomerase VI relaxes supercoiled DNA and introduces supercoils.

Aminoglycoside 3'-phosphotransferases are bacterial enzymes that confer resistance to aminoglycoside antibiotics by phosphorylating them, thus inactivating the drug. These enzymes are structurally and functionally homologous to eukaryotic protein kinases and possess an active site between their N-terminal and C-terminal domains.

Pectate lyase is an enzyme that degrades pectate in plant tissues, contributing to maceration and soft rotting. It contains a parallel β-helix folding motif and is highly expressed during pollen development, potentially aiding pollen tube growth.

RuvA is a DNA helicase subunit that binds Holliday junctions, facilitating their isomerization and base pair rearrangements during homologous recombination in prokaryotes. Its interaction with RuvB regulates branch migration.

This protein family includes enzymes involved in fatty acid synthesis and enoyl-CoA hydration, impacting mitochondrial membrane lipid composition, energy supply, and biopolyester production. Some members function as 3-hydroxyacyl-acyl-carrier-protein dehydratases, crucial for mitochondrial fatty acid synthesis and plant development.

The OAR domain is a 14-amino acid motif found in some homeodomain proteins, potentially involved in transactivation, protein-protein interactions, or DNA binding, though its precise function remains unclear.

Nitroreductases are FMN/FAD-dependent enzymes that reduce nitro compounds using NAD(P)H and are involved in oxidative stress defense. In yeast, they reduce compounds like 4-nitroquinoline-N-oxide (4-NQO).
This group contains phospholipid biosynthesis proteins, such as E. coli PlsX, which is essential for phospholipid synthesis but has an unknown enzymatic function.

This protein family maintains the structure and function of cilia and flagella by regulating dynein arm assembly, axoneme maintenance, and microtubule sliding. Some members also regulate ciliogenesis and mitotic spindle organization.

This protein family includes MtrB, a component of the tetrahydromethanopterin S-methyltransferase complex in archaeal methanogens, which catalyzes a transmethylation reaction coupled to sodium ion transport.

The Sodium:Solute Symporter (SSF) family uses sodium gradients to transport diverse substrates across cell membranes, including vitamins, monocarboxylates, and inorganic ions, and plays roles in nutrient uptake, thyroid function, and neuronal energy status. Some members also regulate cancer cell proliferation.

CLIP domains are regulatory motifs found in arthropod serine proteases that maintain the protease in its zymogen form until activation. These domains, characterized by a unique "paper clip" shape and stabilized by multiple disulfide bonds, are located N-terminal to the protease domain and influence its activity.

Cyclins are eukaryotic proteins that regulate cell division cycles and gene transcription by activating cyclin-dependent kinases (CDKs) and modulating RNA polymerase II activity. They participate in various processes, including pre-mRNA splicing and transcriptional elongation.

Gamma-glutamyl phosphate reductase (GPR) catalyzes a key step in proline biosynthesis by reducing L-glutamate 5-phosphate. It exists as a monofunctional enzyme in bacteria and yeast, and as a C-terminal domain within the bifunctional enzyme P5CS in plants and mammals.
The PHO-4 transporter family includes phosphate permeases and transporters from various organisms, impacting phosphate uptake and bacterial growth.
Creatinine amidohydrolase (creatininase) catalyzes the reversible conversion of creatinine to creatine.

Glycosyltransferase 8 family enzymes synthesize polysaccharides and glycoconjugates, including cell wall components and bacterial lipopolysaccharides, using activated sugar donors. They exhibit glucosyl- and galactosyltransferase activities and are crucial for cell wall integrity and pathogenicity.

RBCC/TRIM proteins, found in metazoa, participate in diverse intracellular processes, functioning within large protein complexes and exhibiting ubiquitin ligase activity. A subset of these proteins, along with some non-RBCC proteins, contain a COS domain, predicted to form two α-helical coils.

RING-type zinc finger domains are protein interaction motifs that bind two zinc atoms and function as E3 ubiquitin-protein ligases, mediating protein-protein interactions in diverse biological processes. They are characterized by a unique "cross-brace" motif and variations in cysteine/histidine patterns.
This entry describes a Cas5 protein family member (hmari subtype) found in certain bacteria and archaea, which functions as part of the CRISPR-Cas defense system against foreign DNA.

The Frizzled (Fz) domain, also known as cysteine-rich domain (CRD), is an extracellular domain found in transmembrane receptors and other proteins, mediating Wnt ligand binding and signaling. Its structure is predominantly α-helical with conserved cysteines forming disulfide bonds.

This protein family contains a RING-type zinc finger domain, characteristic of E3 ubiquitin ligases, and functions in transferring ubiquitin to target proteins, leading to proteasomal degradation or altered protein function.
NADH-dependent nitrate reductases in plants and fungi contain molybdopterin and FAD-binding domains and are involved in nitrate assimilation and nitric oxide synthesis.

The Stf3 family comprises enzymes involved in sulfomenaquinone (SMK) biosynthesis, catalyzing the sulfonation of a menaquinone precursor using PAPS. This process yields sulfomenaquinone, a virulence-modulating compound found in the outer envelope of mycobacteria.

This family comprises E3 ubiquitin ligases that mediate the degradation of target proteins, including transcription factor cofactors, thereby modulating gene expression. They are also involved in telomere length regulation, X chromosome inactivation, protein complex stoichiometry, and neuronal development.

CheW-like domains are structural motifs found in bacterial chemotaxis signaling proteins, mediating protein-protein interactions within two-component systems. They consist of two beta-sheet subdomains that form a hydrophobic core.

Histidine kinases are key components of two-component signaling systems, mediating signal transduction through autophosphorylation and phosphotransfer. They typically consist of a ligand-binding domain and a kinase domain, with the kinase domain facilitating phosphotransfer.
The N-terminal domain of bacterial RecO possesses an OB-fold structure that binds both single- and double-stranded DNA via backbone interactions.

This family includes fungal C-8 sterol isomerases, which catalyze a key step in ergosterol biosynthesis, and mammalian sigma-1 receptors, endoplasmic reticulum proteins that bind steroid hormones and various drugs, potentially influencing NMDA receptor function and other systems.

O-Glycosyl hydrolases are enzymes that hydrolyze glycosidic bonds between carbohydrates or between a carbohydrate and a non-carbohydrate moiety. This entry represents the N-terminal catalytic domain of glucosyltransferases, which transfer glucopyranosyl units from sucrose and also contain a C-terminal glucan-binding domain.

Snf8/Vps22p/Eap30 is a subunit of the ESCRT-II complex, which is essential for sorting ubiquitinated transmembrane proteins from endosomes to lysosomes/vacuoles via the MVB pathway. ESCRT complexes mediate the budding and sorting of cargo into luminal vesicles.

Monocarboxylate Transporters (MCTs) facilitate proton-linked transport of monocarboxylates like lactate and pyruvate across the plasma membrane, contributing to pH regulation, metabolic homeostasis, and hormone distribution. They function via symport or antiport mechanisms and also mediate efflux of toxins.
AFT1 and AFT2 are iron-regulated transcription factors that control genes involved in iron homeostasis.
The ER membrane protein complex (EMC) is a multi-subunit complex involved in protein folding in the ER and lipid transfer to mitochondria. It includes EMC8/NOC4 homologs in various organisms.

Glutamyl endopeptidases cleave peptide bonds at glutamate and aspartate residues, playing roles in bacterial colonization by processing adherence factors and potentially evading the host immune response. Some members degrade extracellular matrix proteins and are regulated by inhibitors.

This entry describes E3 ubiquitin ligases, such as SP1 and SPL1, that regulate protein import into chloroplasts by ubiquitinating TOC components and promoting their degradation. It also includes some uncharacterized bacterial proteins.

This acyltransferase domain is found in bacterial and eukaryotic proteins and catalyzes the transfer of acyl groups, contributing to processes like peptidoglycan modification for lysozyme resistance and lipo-chitin oligosaccharide production.
DsrD protein contains a winged-helix motif, similar to DNA-binding proteins, suggesting a role in regulating the transcription or translation of genes involved in dissimilatory sulfite reduction.

ABC transporters are ATP-dependent proteins that translocate diverse substrates across membranes, mediating processes like bacteriocin export, antibiotic resistance, cofactor assembly, and sporulation. The functional diversity within the family suggests potential roles yet to be discovered.
Eukaryotic acetyltransferases, including choline, carnitine, and palmitoyltransferases, form a family of enzymes involved in neurotransmitter biosynthesis, fatty acid metabolism, and transport.
This entry represents uncharacterized proteins from Mycobacterium tuberculosis with unknown functions.
The tify domain, found in land plants, is a variable 36-amino acid motif named after its conserved TIFF/YXG pattern.

SctL stator family proteins regulate type III secretion systems (T3SS) for injecting effector proteins into host cells, while FliH family proteins are involved in flagella assembly for bacterial motility; both contribute to bacterial survival and pathogenicity.

The exocyst complex, composed of eight Sec proteins, mediates secretory vesicle tethering during exocytosis. Exo84, a subunit of the exocyst, also participates in pre-mRNA splicing and epithelial polarity.
Adenosine deaminases are purine metabolism enzymes that convert adenosine to inosine, playing a role in the breakdown of adenosine and turnover of nucleic acids.
This protein family participates in acetoin catabolism, likely functioning as kinases within the aco operon.

EFHD1/2 are EF-hand domain-containing proteins; EFHD1 may sense mitochondrial Ca2+ to activate mitoflashes, while EFHD2 is a Ca2+ binding adapter protein involved in cellular functions and B-cell apoptosis regulation.

This aminotransferase-like domain, found in plant proteins like MAIN, is implicated in maintaining genomic stability, possibly through control of heterochromatic elements. It shares similarity with a DNA-binding motif in transposases, suggesting a transposon-derived origin.
Oep21 is an ATP-regulated anion channel found in the outer chloroplast membrane of plants and green algae.
TARBP1 is a methyltransferase that modifies RNA, including tRNAs, and binds to HIV-1 TAR RNA, potentially influencing viral transcription.
This entry describes putative methyltransferases like human TARBP1 and yeast Trm3. TARBP1 binds HIV-1 TAR RNA, while Trm3 methylates tRNA and is involved in oxidative stress response.

This family includes sugar and myo-inositol transporters, integral membrane proteins with 12 transmembrane domains found in both prokaryotes and eukaryotes. These transporters facilitate the binding and transport of various carbohydrates, alcohols, and acids.

TEF/Scalloped proteins are transcriptional factors with a TEA/ATTS DNA-binding domain that interact with Vestigial-like proteins (Vgl) to regulate development and differentiation in a tissue-specific manner. The Vgl-TEF/Scalloped complex regulates gene expression, impacting processes like wing development, muscle differentiation, and potentially cancer progression.
The C-terminal domain of fungal SWT1 proteins, similar to HEPN domains, is an RNA endonuclease involved in mRNA quality control and contains multiple alpha-helices.

Archaemetzincins are archaeal zinc-dependent peptidases belonging to the MEROPS family M54, characterized by a metal-binding site, often containing an HEXXH motif, that activates water molecules for proteolysis.

Peptidase family M54 (archaemetzincins) are zinc-dependent aminopeptidases found in archaea, bacteria, and eukaryotes, characterized by a metzincin motif and a second zinc-binding site. Some members have evolved into zinc-binding transcription factors.

This protein family includes AFG1, LACE1, and ZapE, which function as ATPases involved in mitochondrial maintenance (AFG1, LACE1) and bacterial cell division (ZapE). LACE1 also mediates the degradation of complex IV subunits and is required for normal respiratory chain activity.

Signal peptidase complex subunit 3 (Spc3) is a component of the signal peptidase complex (SPC), which cleaves signal sequences from nascent proteins during translocation across the endoplasmic reticulum membrane.
This protein family facilitates copper efflux, likely contributing to copper resistance, and belongs to the major facilitator superfamily of transmembrane transporters.
The GidA-associated domain is the C-terminal domain of MnmG, a tRNA modification enzyme in bacteria and mitochondria, and it is suggested to bind the D-stem of tRNA and interact with protein MnmE.
The C-terminal subdomain of MnmG (GidA) is a conserved helical bundle that binds tRNA's D-stem and interacts with MnmE in the tRNA modification complex.

Metallo-dependent hydrolases catalyze diverse hydrolytic reactions, including degradation of dihydropyrimidines and hydrolysis of hydantoin derivatives, impacting cytoskeletal dynamics, axon guidance, and neuronal development. Some members participate in pyrimidine degradation and regulation of dendrite outgrowth.

1-Deoxy-D-xylulose-5-phosphate synthase (DXP synthase) is a thiamine diphosphate-dependent enzyme involved in terpenoid biosynthesis, catalyzing the condensation of pyruvate and glyceraldehyde 3-phosphate to produce 1-deoxy-D-xylulose-5-phosphate. It is related to transketolase and utilizes thiamine pyrophosphate as a cofactor.

This protein family forms the hexameric building blocks of bacterial microcompartments (BMCs), organelles that encapsulate enzymes and control metabolite diffusion. These proteins are essential for BMC structural integrity and facilitate metabolic processes like substrate degradation and carbon fixation.
APQ12 family proteins are conserved fungal proteins involved in nuclear pore assembly and mRNA export. They regulate membrane fluidity, impacting the localization of nucleoporins.

This domain is found in folate receptors, which bind folates and mediate their transport into cells, and in hedgehog-interacting proteins; it is also present in a riboflavin-binding protein involved in riboflavin transport.

Oleosins are plant proteins that stabilize lipid storage bodies (oil bodies) by preventing coalescence and providing lipase anchorage during seedling growth. They consist of a conserved hydrophobic domain that interacts with lipids, flanked by variable hydrophilic and amphipathic regions.
This protein family mediates endomembrane trafficking by forming complexes that modulate the Golgi network and endosome function, linking post-Golgi traffic to the actin cytoskeleton.

This protein family comprises components of the mitoribosome, which synthesizes mitochondrial proteins essential for the respiratory chain. Mitoribosomes associate with the inner membrane, enabling cotranslational integration of transmembrane subunits required for mitochondrial function.

Prephenate dehydrogenase (PDHG) is an enzyme involved in tyrosine biosynthesis, catalyzing the conversion of chorismate to hydroxyphenylpyruvate or pyruvate. This entry also includes related proteins such as 3-phosphoshikimate 1-carboxyvinyltransferase.

This protein family comprises potassium-binding proteins with BON and LysM domains, crucial for growth in high-potassium environments, and likely act as cytoplasmic potassium sensors undergoing conformational changes upon ion binding. While primarily binding potassium, these proteins can also accommodate larger alkali metal ions and ammonium.

Longin domains are conserved α/β fold structures involved in membrane dynamics, found in proteins like FUZ, MON1 (a GEF for Rab7), and HPS1 (a GEF for Rab32/38). These domains participate in vesicular trafficking, organelle biogenesis, and ciliogenesis.

Longin domains are conserved α/β-fold motifs involved in membrane dynamics, found in proteins like FUZ, MON1 (a Rab7 GEF), and HPS1 (a Rab32/38 GEF). They participate in vesicular trafficking, organelle biogenesis, and ciliogenesis.

Longin domains are conserved eukaryotic motifs involved in membrane dynamics, found in proteins like FUZ, MON1, and HPS1. These domains, characterized by an α/β fold, participate in vesicular trafficking, organelle biogenesis, and ciliogenesis.
This domain, found in fungal MTC6 proteins, is predicted to form a partial TIM-barrel and may function in telomere capping. It shows similarity to Phosphatidylinositol-specific phospholipase C.

The N-terminal region of CAMSAP proteins contains two calponin-homology (CH) domains, which, along with a C-terminal CKK domain, are crucial for microtubule binding, organization, and protection against depolymerization.

The calponin homology (CH) domain is an actin-binding motif found in proteins involved in cytoskeletal organization, including actin cross-linkers, F-actin binding proteins, and proteins regulating contractility. It typically consists of two CH domains that together form an actin-binding domain (ABD), although single CH domains can also be found in proteins with diverse functions.

This VHS/ENTH-like domain, found in proteins like human Tepsin, is an evolutionarily conserved module involved in clathrin-mediated endocytosis, binding inositol phospholipids and proteins to promote clathrin coat formation. Tepsin interacts with the AP-4 complex and potentially participates in protein trafficking at the trans-Golgi network.

CLE14 is a plant signal peptide that regulates cell fate by repressing root apical meristem maintenance and promoting root meristem differentiation through the CLV2/CRN complex, ultimately inhibiting root growth.

DnaG primases synthesize short RNA primers for DNA replication, with bacterial and phage primases forming one group and eukaryotic/archaeal primases another; bacterial DnaG interacts with DnaB helicase to initiate Okazaki fragments on the lagging strand.

The transforming protein Qin, a homolog of FOXG1, is a winged helix transcription factor involved in brain development and telencephalon formation, acting as a transcriptional repressor. Mutations in FOXG1 are associated with FOXG1-related encephalopathy, characterized by microcephaly, developmental delays, and other neurological issues.

The phosphoglycerate mutase family catalyzes phosphoglycerate interconversion in glycolysis and gluconeogenesis, while some members exhibit phosphatase activity in biosynthesis. Certain proteins have evolved specialized functions in carbohydrate metabolism, serine biosynthesis, or vitamin B12 intermediate hydrolysis.

SH3 domains are small protein modules found in intracellular and membrane-associated proteins that bind proline-rich motifs, mediating protein-protein interactions and the assembly of protein complexes. They consist of a characteristic fold of anti-parallel β-sheets, forming a hydrophobic pocket for ligand binding.
This entry describes the dynamin-type G domain, which binds guanine nucleotides.

LAZY proteins regulate plant gravitropism by influencing auxin distribution in roots and shoots, thereby affecting root system architecture. They function in gravity-sensing cells (statocytes) to mediate the directional response to gravity.

This protein family acts as molecular chaperones, interacting with HSP70 to facilitate protein folding and regulate heat shock-induced transcription. Some members also participate in cell division, translation, and axonemal structure/motility.

Cytochrome c oxidase (CcO) is a transmembrane protein complex that catalyzes the reduction of oxygen and pumps protons across the membrane, essential for cellular respiration in eukaryotes and bacteria. Subunit IV, found in eukaryotes, regulates enzyme activity through ATP binding.

Dictyostelium CAD family proteins mediate calcium-dependent cell-cell adhesion via their N-terminal segment, which is crucial for cell assembly during early development, while the C-terminal domain anchors them to the cell membrane.

Longin domains are conserved α/β fold structures involved in membrane dynamics regulation and found in proteins like INTU, CCZ1, and HPS4. They function as GEFs for Rab GTPases in vesicular trafficking and ciliogenesis.

Longin domains are conserved α/β fold motifs involved in membrane dynamics regulation, found in proteins like MON1/CCZ1 (Ypt7/Rab7 GEF) and HPS1/HPS4 (Rab32/38 GEF). This entry represents the third Longin domain found in CCZ1, INTU and HPS4 proteins.
Maf1 is a repressor of RNA polymerase III that inhibits TFIIIB assembly in response to stress and targets the initiation factor TFIIIB. Its structure has been solved.
The CSD2 domain of E. coli RNase II, a non-canonical cold-shock domain, contributes to RNA binding despite lacking typical sequence motifs.
S1 domains, related to OB-fold family and cold shock proteins, are RNA-binding motifs found in RNA metabolism proteins, characterized by a beta-barrel structure with a conserved RNA-binding site.

Protein virilizer regulates mRNA splicing, particularly of sex-related transcripts like Sxl and Ubx, and is essential for viability, sex determination, and dosage compensation, as demonstrated in Drosophila.

VSR endonucleases are bacterial and archaeal enzymes that repair TG mismatches resulting from cytosine deamination by cleaving the DNA backbone and favoring the G-containing strand. They share structural similarity with restriction endonucleases.

Large ribosomal subunit protein cL38 (PSRP-6) is a plastid-specific ribosomal protein found in the 50S subunit of plant ribosomes, which are responsible for mRNA-directed protein synthesis. Ribosomal proteins like PSRP-6 stabilize rRNA structure and may have evolved to streamline protein synthesis.
Glycine N-acyltransferases are mitochondrial enzymes that catalyze the transfer of acyl groups from acyl-CoA to glycine, producing N-acylglycines with diverse aliphatic and aromatic acyl donors.
APC10/DOC domains are ubiquitin ligase subunits found in the anaphase-promoting complex (APC) and other proteins, mediating ubiquitination via a beta-sandwich 'jellyroll' fold.
This family contains a domain found in CobN proteins, which are involved in cobalamin biosynthesis, and magnesium protoporphyrin chelatase, an enzyme participating in chlorophyll biosynthesis.
This protein family catalyzes the specific hydrolysis of UTP to UMP and diphosphate, functioning in pyrimidine salvage and regulating intracellular UTP and UMP levels.
MTOPVIB is a structural homolog of archaeal topo VIB that forms a complex with SPO11 to catalyze meiotic DNA double-strand breaks.
This TPR domain, found in bacterial cytochrome c-type biogenesis proteins like CcmH and CycH, is involved in protein-protein interactions during cytochrome maturation.
Dihydrodipicolinate reductase (DHDPR) catalyzes a key step in lysine biosynthesis, reducing dihydrodipicolinate to tetrahydrodipicolinate using NAD(P)H, and may also function as a dehydratase.
Plant glycerolipid A1 lipases (PLIPs) are plastid phospholipases that release fatty acids from chloroplast phosphatidylglycerol, impacting seed oil and jasmonic acid biosynthesis.

QuiC is a 3-dehydroshikimate dehydratase that converts 3-dehydroshikimate to protocatechuate, an intermediate in quinate and shikimate degradation, and contains a sugar phosphate isomerase-like domain and a hydroxyphenylpyruvate dioxygenase-like domain.

The irregular helical bundle (IHB) domain of Nup120 is a conserved component of the nuclear pore complex (NPC) that is involved in macromolecular transport and NPC organization. Nup120 interacts with other nucleoporins and its phosphorylation may regulate the association of the Nup107-160 subcomplex with the NPC.

HEAT repeats in fungal Nup120 form a structural component of the nuclear pore complex (NPC), which mediates macromolecular transport across the nuclear envelope and interacts with other nucleoporins to regulate mRNA export.

This protein family includes transcriptional regulators and deubiquitinases involved in chromatin remodeling, DNA damage response, and cell cycle progression. They maintain genome stability by modulating ubiquitination and influencing neuronal differentiation and gene expression.

Cytochrome P450s in fungi are monooxygenases that catalyze the oxidation of organic substrates, playing a key role in the biosynthesis of diverse secondary metabolites like mycotoxins and antibiotics. These enzymes contribute to the structural diversification of natural products, leading to compounds with various bioactivities.
AreA proteins are GATA transcription factors with a conserved N-terminus that regulate nitrogen metabolism.
This domain is found in plant E3 ubiquitin ligases like RKP, which targets proteins such as KRP1 and KRP2 for degradation via ubiquitination.
This region contains TPR-like repeats found in E3 ubiquitin ligases such as RNF123 and RKP, which regulate cell cycle proteins and antiviral signaling via ubiquitination and proteasomal degradation.
Svp26/Tex261 proteins are ER membrane proteins; yeast Svp26 acts as an ER exit adaptor for mannosyltransferases Mnt2 and Mnt3, while the function of Tex261 is unknown.

RRM MRD1 family proteins participate in RNA processing and ribosome biogenesis, including pre-rRNA processing, ribosome subunit assembly, and potentially RNA splicing. These functions are essential for protein synthesis and pre-implantation embryo development.

This domain is found in proteins involved in DNA-related processes like transcription regulation, DNA repair, and chromatin unwinding, often functioning as an ATPase that disrupts histone-DNA interactions.
Eukaryotic D-xylulose kinases (XK) catalyze the ATP-dependent phosphorylation of D-xylulose to D-xylulose 5-phosphate (X5P).

GID4/10 proteins are substrate-recognition components of the GID E3 ubiquitin ligase complex, targeting proteins for degradation in response to environmental cues like catabolite degradation, osmotic stress, or heat stress. They mediate the ubiquitination of specific substrates, such as FBP1 or ART2, for proteasomal degradation.

This protein family functions as seed storage reserves, providing carbon and nitrogen for seedlings post-germination, and some members exhibit lectin-like and antimicrobial activities for plant defense.

Transmembrane ATPases are membrane-bound ion transporters that utilize ATP hydrolysis (or proton gradients in reverse) to move protons across membranes. P-ATPases, a class of these enzymes, transport various ions and phospholipids and are found in bacteria and eukaryotic membranes.

This protein family functions in pre-mRNA splicing as part of the U1 snRNP complex, recognizing the 5' splice site via an RRM domain. Some members participate in minor splicing and regulate cellular processes like oogenesis and sporulation.

This protein family comprises calcium-binding proteins that regulate muscle contraction via troponin complexes and modulate various enzymes and cellular processes through calcium signaling. Some members also influence myosin ATPase activity and may be involved in toxin secretion and cellular control.

2-oxoacid:acceptor oxidoreductase alpha subunits catalyze CoA-dependent oxidative decarboxylation of 2-oxoacids, utilizing thiamine diphosphate (TPP) and potentially replacing the lipoate-based 2-oxoglutarate dehydrogenase system in some organisms.
This entry describes the C-terminal domain of antitoxins in type II toxin-antitoxin systems, which neutralizes the associated toxin by blocking its active site.

The Xre N-terminal domain is a helix-turn-helix (HTH) DNA-binding domain, similar to the Cro repressor, found in antitoxin components of type II toxin-antitoxin (TA) systems. It binds DNA and is associated with a toxin-binding domain.

CHCHD2/10 are homologous proteins in the mitochondrial intermembrane space that regulate mitochondrial dynamics, cristae organization, and respiration. CHCHD2 acts as a transcription factor, while mutations in both proteins are linked to neurodegenerative diseases.

Mannose-1-phosphate guanyltransferases catalyze GDP-mannose formation, a precursor for glycoprotein and glycolipid glycosylation essential for cell wall synthesis in fungi and ascorbate biosynthesis in plants. Some subunits also regulate enzymatic activity via feedback inhibition.
This protein family catalyzes the aerobic insertion of cobalt into the corrin ring during adenosylcobalamin biosynthesis.

Immunoglobulin domains are structural motifs found in diverse proteins, including cell surface receptors and adhesion molecules, and are involved in cell-cell recognition, immune response, and muscle structure. They exhibit a conserved Greek-key β-sandwich structure and are classified into V-set, C1-set, C2-set, and I-set types based on sequence and structural variations.

MAP Kinase Phosphatases (MKPs) are dual-specificity phosphatases that deactivate MAP kinases by dephosphorylating tyrosine and threonine residues, utilizing a conserved active site motif. MKPs interact with MAP kinases via a Kinase Interaction Motif (KIM), exhibiting specificity towards different MAP kinase family members.

FMR1/FMRP family proteins are RNA-binding proteins with KH and RGG domains that regulate mRNA splicing, stability, transport, and translation, impacting neuronal development and synaptic plasticity. They also contain Agenet-like domains, which recognize trimethylated peptides and histone H3 'Lys-9' methylation marks, potentially influencing transcriptional silencing and DNA methylation.

Domain II of alpha-D-phosphohexomutase enzymes exhibits an α/β/α topology and is found in enzymes catalyzing phosphoryl transfer on sugar substrates, including phosphoglucomutases and related mutases involved in glucose and carbohydrate metabolism.

uL10 family proteins are essential ribosomal components that form part of the ribosomal stalk, which recruits translation factors and enhances GTPase activity during protein synthesis. They are crucial for ribosome function and contribute to the peptidyl transferase center.

EGF domains are extracellular motifs, often calcium-binding, found in membrane-bound and secreted proteins, characterized by conserved cysteines forming disulfide bonds and a compact beta-sheet structure.

The N-terminal domain of 4'-phosphopantetheinyl transferase enzymes, found also in enterobactin synthase component D (EntD), catalyzes the transfer of the 4'-phosphopantetheine moiety to activate domains involved in enterobactin assembly and virulence.
FliW binds flagellin to regulate its polymerization and flagellum assembly. It also participates in a feedback loop that promotes flagellin production.
This conserved domain is found in bacterial polymorphic toxins, preceding the toxin module.
This entry describes (-)-alpha-amorphene synthase, a terpene synthase that catalyzes the cyclization of farnesyl diphosphate (FPP) to alpha-amorphene. It specifically uses (2E,6E)-FPP as a substrate.

The PDGF/VEGF family of growth factors regulates angiogenesis, vasculogenesis, and endothelial cell growth by stimulating cell proliferation, migration, and survival. Some members also modulate vascular permeability in physiological processes and contribute to venom dispersion in snakes.

SRSF proteins are RNA-binding factors with SR domains that regulate splicing, transcription, and mRNA export, influencing gene expression, cell cycle progression, and stress responses. They participate in spliceosome assembly and ribosome biogenesis.
Heparin-sulfate lyase (HepC) is a bacterial enzyme that cleaves heparan sulfate polysaccharides, providing nutrients for microorganisms. It specifically targets heparan sulfate-rich regions.

KhpA is an RNA-binding protein that forms a complex with KhpB to regulate RNA expression and stimulate cell wall biosynthesis. KhpA also plays a role in peptidoglycan homeostasis and cell length regulation.
This entry represents plant LORELEI-like proteins (LRE/LLGs) that act as chaperones and co-receptors for receptor kinases involved in plant growth, reproduction, and fertilization.

This entry describes bacterial and fungal N-methyltransferases, including histidine-specific methyltransferases (EgtD) that produce hercynine, 4-dimethylallyltryptophan N-methyltransferases involved in ergot alkaloid biosynthesis, and N-methyltransferases (dtpB) required for ditryptophenaline biosynthesis.

2P-domain channels are potassium channels that regulate cell excitability and resting membrane potential, and are modulated by factors like oxygen levels and pH. They function as background K+ channels with a characteristic four transmembrane segment topology.
RimP (YlxS) is required for the maturation of the 30S ribosomal subunit and efficient production of functional ribosomes.
